direct-proportional.name: Direct Proportional Function
direct-proportional.description: A straight line through the origin whose steepness is set by k.
direct-proportional.explanation: Direct proportion means y changes at a constant rate with x. The graph is always a line through the origin; only its slope changes. Larger |k| means a steeper line; the sign of k flips the line between the rising and falling diagonals.
examples: Distance vs time at constant speed (d = vt).
Total price vs quantity at a fixed unit price.

k.label: Slope
k.explanation: Controls the steepness and direction. k>0 rises, k<0 falls.

linear.name: Linear Function
linear.description: A straight line y = kx + b with adjustable slope and intercept.
linear.explanation: k controls the slope (steepness and direction) while b shifts the whole line up or down, setting where it crosses the y-axis. Changing b slides the line vertically without changing its tilt.
examples: Taxi fare = base fare + rate × distance.

k.label: Slope
k.explanation: The slope: how fast y changes per unit x.

b.label: y-intercept
b.explanation: Where the line crosses the y-axis.

quadratic.name: Quadratic Function
quadratic.description: A parabola y = ax² + bx + c with vertex and axis of symmetry.
quadratic.explanation: The sign of a decides whether the parabola opens upward (a>0) or downward (a<0); the magnitude of a controls how narrow it is. The vertex is the turning point and the axis of symmetry is the vertical line through it. c is simply the height where the curve crosses the y-axis.
examples: Path of a thrown ball (projectile motion).
Area of a rectangle as a function of one side with fixed perimeter.

a.label: a
a.explanation: Opening direction & width. a>0 opens up, a<0 opens down; larger |a| is narrower.

b.label: b
b.explanation: Shifts the axis of symmetry left/right (together with a).

inverse-proportional.name: Inverse Proportional Function
inverse-proportional.description: A hyperbola y = k/x with the axes as asymptotes.
inverse-proportional.explanation: As x grows the product xy stays equal to k, so y shrinks toward 0 — the curve hugs both axes (its asymptotes) but never touches them. The two branches sit in opposite quadrants depending on the sign of k.
examples: Speed vs travel time for a fixed distance.
Pressure vs volume of a gas at constant temperature (Boyle's law).

k.label: Constant
k.explanation: k>0 puts the branches in quadrants I & III; k<0 in II & IV.

absolute-value.name: Absolute Value Function
absolute-value.description: A V-shaped graph y = a|x − h| + k.
absolute-value.explanation: The absolute value forces all outputs above the vertex, producing a symmetric V. (h, k) is the corner of the V; a scales the arms and, if negative, flips the V upside-down.

a.label: a
a.explanation: Steepness and direction of the V (a<0 makes it open downward).

power.name: Power Function
power.description: y = xᵃ — its shape depends on the exponent a.
power.explanation: A power function raises x to a fixed exponent. Integer exponents are defined for all x, but fractional exponents need x ≥ 0 to stay real, so the curve only appears on the right side there. Even exponents are symmetric about the y-axis; odd exponents are symmetric about the origin.

a.label: Exponent
a.explanation: a=1 line, a=2 parabola, a=3 cubic, a=0.5 square root, a<0 hyperbola-like.

formulas.label: Note
formulas.description: Negative bases have no real value for fractional exponents.

exponential.name: Exponential Function
exponential.description: y = aˣ — rapid growth (a>1) or decay (0<a<1).
exponential.explanation: Exponential functions multiply by the base a for each step in x. When a>1 the output explodes upward; when 0<a<1 it decays toward the horizontal asymptote y = 0. All of them pass through (0, 1) because a⁰ = 1.

a.label: Base
a.explanation: a>1 grows; 0<a<1 decays. Every curve passes through (0, 1).

logarithmic.name: Logarithmic Function
logarithmic.description: y = logₐ(x) — the inverse of the exponential function.
logarithmic.explanation: The logarithm answers 'a to what power gives x?'. It is the mirror image of y = aˣ across the line y = x, so its domain is x > 0 and it has a vertical asymptote at x = 0. All curves pass through (1, 0).

a.label: Base
a.explanation: a>1 rises; 0<a<1 falls. Every curve passes through (1, 0).

sine.name: Sine Function
sine.description: y = A·sin(Bx + C) + D with amplitude, period and shifts.
sine.explanation: A stretches the wave vertically (amplitude); B compresses it horizontally, setting the period T = 2π/|B|; C slides it left/right (phase shift −C/B); D raises or lowers the midline.
examples: Tides and daylight hours over a year.

A.label: Amplitude
A.explanation: Half the height between max and min.

B.label: Frequency
B.explanation: Larger B squeezes the wave (shorter period).

D.label: Vertical shift
D.explanation: Moves the midline up or down.

cosine.name: Cosine Function
cosine.description: y = A·cos(Bx + C) + D — a sine wave shifted by a quarter period.
cosine.explanation: Cosine has the same shape as sine but starts at its maximum: cos(x) = sin(x + π/2). The parameters mean exactly the same things — amplitude A, period 2π/|B|, phase shift, and vertical shift D.
examples: Horizontal position of a point on a rotating wheel.

A.label: Amplitude
A.explanation: Half the height between max and min.

B.label: Frequency
B.explanation: Larger B squeezes the wave (shorter period).

D.label: Vertical shift
D.explanation: Moves the midline up or down.

tangent.name: Tangent Function
tangent.description: y = A·tan(Bx + C) + D with repeating vertical asymptotes.
tangent.explanation: Tangent repeats every π and shoots off to ±∞ at each asymptote, where cos = 0. The graph is drawn as separate branches — it is never connected across an asymptote. B sets how often the asymptotes occur.
examples: Slope of a line at a given angle.
Modeling rapid blow-ups near a limit.

B.label: Frequency
B.explanation: Controls the spacing of the asymptotes (period π/|B|).

D.label: Vertical shift
D.explanation: Moves the curve up or down.

piecewise.name: Piecewise Function
piecewise.description: Different rules on different intervals, joined at a breakpoint.
piecewise.explanation: A piecewise function applies a different formula on each interval of its domain. Here the left piece (x < 0) uses −sx and the right piece (x ≥ 0) uses sx. The two pieces are drawn separately and only meet at the breakpoint x = 0.

scale.label: Slope scale
scale.explanation: Scales both arms so you can watch the pieces change together.

formulas.label: Definition
formulas.description: Two linear rules meeting at x = 0.

circle.name: Circle
circle.description: All points at distance r from a center (h, k).
circle.explanation: A circle is the set of points a fixed distance r (the radius) from its center (h, k). Moving h and k slides the whole circle; changing r grows or shrinks it. Its circumference and area depend only on r.

r.label: Radius
r.explanation: Distance from the center to the edge.

ellipse.name: Ellipse
ellipse.description: A stretched circle with semi-axes a and b.
ellipse.explanation: An ellipse is a circle scaled by different amounts along the two axes. a and b are the semi-major and semi-minor axes (whichever is larger is 'major'). When a = b the ellipse becomes a circle. Its area is π·a·b.

a.label: Semi-axis a
a.explanation: Half the width along the x-axis.

b.label: Semi-axis b
b.explanation: Half the height along the y-axis.

hyperbola.name: Hyperbola
hyperbola.description: Two opposing branches with straight-line asymptotes.
hyperbola.explanation: A hyperbola has two mirror-image branches that open left and right, each approaching the asymptotes y = ±(b/a)x without touching them. The simple inverse-proportional curve y = k/x is a hyperbola too — just rotated so the axes themselves are its asymptotes.
examples: The path of an object escaping gravity.
Shadow of a lamp cone on a wall.

a.label: a
a.explanation: Sets the vertices at (±a, 0) and the asymptote slope.

b.label: b
b.explanation: Together with a sets the asymptote slope ±b/a.

parabola-conic.name: Parabola (Conic)
parabola-conic.description: The conic y² = 2px with a focus and directrix.
parabola-conic.explanation: Every point on a parabola is equidistant from a fixed point (the focus) and a fixed line (the directrix). For y² = 2px the curve opens to the right, the focus sits at (p/2, 0), and the directrix is the vertical line x = −p/2. Larger p makes a wider opening.

p.label: Parameter p
p.explanation: Distance-related parameter; p>0 opens to the right.

cuboid.name: Cuboid (Rectangular Prism)
cuboid.description: A box with length, width and height.
cuboid.explanation: A cuboid has three pairs of identical rectangular faces. Its volume multiplies the three edge lengths; its surface area sums the areas of all six faces.

cube.name: Cube
cube.description: A cuboid with all edges equal.
cube.explanation: A cube is the most symmetric cuboid: all 12 edges are equal and all 6 faces are congruent squares. Doubling the edge multiplies the volume by 8.

cylinder.name: Cylinder
cylinder.description: Two circular bases joined by a curved surface.
cylinder.explanation: Unrolling a cylinder's curved side gives a rectangle of width 2πr and height h, which is why the lateral area is 2πrh. Adding the two circular ends gives the total surface area.

triangular-prism.name: Triangular Prism
triangular-prism.description: A triangle swept along its length.
triangular-prism.explanation: A prism keeps the same cross-section all along its length, so its volume is simply the base area times the length. Here the base is a triangle of area ½·b·hₜ.

b.label: Triangle base
b.explanation: Base length of the triangular cross-section.

H.label: Prism length
H.explanation: How far the triangle is swept.

quadrangular-prism.name: Quadrangular Prism
quadrangular-prism.description: A rectangular base swept into a prism.
quadrangular-prism.explanation: A quadrangular prism has a rectangular (quadrilateral) base. Like every prism its volume is the base area times the height. With a rectangular base this is the same as a cuboid: B = lw.

pentagonal-prism.name: Pentagonal Prism
pentagonal-prism.description: A regular pentagon swept into a prism.
pentagonal-prism.explanation: The base is a regular pentagon, whose area uses the general regular-polygon formula with n = 5. Multiplying by the height gives the prism's volume.

a.label: Side length
a.explanation: Side length of the regular pentagon.

hexagonal-prism.name: Hexagonal Prism
hexagonal-prism.description: A regular hexagon swept into a prism.
hexagonal-prism.explanation: A regular hexagon is six equilateral triangles, giving the tidy base-area formula (3√3/2)·a². Hexagonal prisms tile space efficiently, which is why bees build hexagonal cells.

a.label: Side length
a.explanation: Side length of the regular hexagon.

triangular-pyramid.name: Triangular Pyramid
triangular-pyramid.description: A regular triangular base tapering to an apex.
triangular-pyramid.explanation: A pyramid's volume is always one third of the prism with the same base and height. Here the base is an equilateral triangle of area (√3/4)·a².

a.label: Base side
a.explanation: Side length of the equilateral base.

h.label: Height
h.explanation: Perpendicular height from base to apex.

quadrangular-pyramid.name: Quadrangular Pyramid
quadrangular-pyramid.description: A rectangular base tapering to an apex.
quadrangular-pyramid.explanation: This is the classic 'Egyptian' pyramid shape: a rectangular base rising to a single apex. Its volume is one third of the matching cuboid (base area × height).

h.label: Height
h.explanation: Perpendicular height from base to apex.

pentagonal-pyramid.name: Pentagonal Pyramid
pentagonal-pyramid.description: A regular pentagon base tapering to an apex.
pentagonal-pyramid.explanation: A pyramid on a regular pentagon. The base area uses the regular-polygon formula with n = 5; the volume is one third of base area times height, like every pyramid.

a.label: Side length
a.explanation: Side length of the regular pentagon base.

h.label: Height
h.explanation: Perpendicular height from base to apex.

hexagonal-pyramid.name: Hexagonal Pyramid
hexagonal-pyramid.description: A regular hexagon base tapering to an apex.
hexagonal-pyramid.explanation: A pyramid on a regular hexagonal base. As always for pyramids, V = ⅓·B·h, with B the hexagon area (3√3/2)·a².

a.label: Side length
a.explanation: Side length of the regular hexagon base.

h.label: Height
h.explanation: Perpendicular height from base to apex.

cone.name: Cone
cone.description: A circular base tapering to a point.
cone.explanation: A cone is a pyramid with a circular base, so its volume is also one third of the matching cylinder. The slant height l (not the vertical height) is what determines the curved lateral surface area πrl.

h.label: Height
h.explanation: Perpendicular height from base to apex.

sphere.name: Sphere
sphere.description: All points at distance r from a center, in 3D.
sphere.explanation: A sphere is the 3D analogue of a circle. Its surface area is exactly four times the area of its 'shadow' circle (4πr²), and its volume grows with the cube of the radius.

r.label: Radius
r.explanation: Distance from the center to the surface.

cone-frustum.name: Frustum of a Cone
cone-frustum.description: A cone with its tip sliced off parallel to the base.
cone-frustum.explanation: A frustum is what remains of a cone after the top is cut off parallel to the base. Its volume blends the two radii with the cross-term Rr. The slant height uses the difference R − r, not R + r.

h.label: Height
h.explanation: Vertical distance between the two circles.

pyramid-frustum.name: Frustum of a Pyramid
pyramid-frustum.description: A regular polygonal pyramid with its top sliced off.
pyramid-frustum.explanation: Slicing the top off a pyramid parallel to its base leaves a frustum with two similar polygonal faces. The volume formula averages the two base areas B₁ and B₂ together with their geometric mean √(B₁B₂).

n.label: Number of sides
n.explanation: Number of sides of the regular polygon base.

aBottom.label: Bottom side
aBottom.explanation: Side length of the larger (bottom) polygon.

aTop.label: Top side
aTop.explanation: Side length of the smaller (top) polygon.

h.label: Height
h.explanation: Vertical distance between the two bases.

house.name: House
house.description: A cuboid body with a triangular-prism roof.
house.explanation: To find the volume of a combined solid, split it into basic solids, compute each, then add. A house is a cuboid (the walls) plus a triangular prism (the roof). Add the two volumes for the total; the contact face where they meet is internal and is not part of the outside surface.

l.label: Length
l.explanation: Width of the house (and roof span).

pencil.name: Pencil
pencil.description: A cylinder body topped by a cone tip.
pencil.explanation: A pencil is a cylinder (the body) with a cone (the sharpened tip) sharing the same radius. The total volume is the cylinder plus the cone. Since both share the circular contact face, no outer surface passes through it.

ht.label: Tip length
ht.explanation: Length of the sharpened cone tip.

ice-cream.name: Ice Cream
ice-cream.description: A cone holding a spherical scoop.
ice-cream.explanation: An ice cream is a cone (point down) with a sphere resting on top. Add the cone and sphere volumes for the total. In reality part of the scoop sits inside the cone's mouth, so a careful answer would subtract that overlap — a good example of when to subtract.

r.label: Cone radius
r.explanation: Radius of the cone's open top.

h.label: Cone height
h.explanation: Length of the cone (tip points down).

water-tower.name: Water Tower
water-tower.description: A cylinder capped with a hemispherical dome.
water-tower.explanation: A water tower is a cylinder with a hemisphere (half a sphere) on top, sharing the same radius. The total volume adds the cylinder and the hemisphere. A hemisphere is exactly half a sphere, so its volume is ⅔πr³.

r.label: Radius
r.explanation: Radius of the cylinder and the dome.

castle.name: Castle
castle.description: A central keep with four corner towers and conical roofs.
castle.explanation: A castle combines several basic solids: a cuboid keep, four cylindrical towers, and four conical roofs. The strategy is the same as always — break it into known pieces, compute each volume, then add them all up. By symmetry the four towers and four roofs are identical, so we multiply by four.

s.label: Keep size
s.explanation: Side length of the square central keep.
